ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general dumpling class; no chicken-specific dumpling subclass exists. Mapped to closest parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKW covers filled dough products including dumplings; chicken-specific variant not separately coded. Closest defensible match used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for chicken dumpling as a food product. Left null to maintain ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of chicken dumplings in Singapore is approximately 4 to 5 pieces, weighing around 150 grams, commonly served as a main dish or dim sum item.

nutrient_source.title: Chicken Dumpling — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input record cr_id G00010773 is sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo with provenance marked canonical and confidence 0.95. All per_100g values are taken directly from this source.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for chicken dumpling in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 55 (low) based on the closest analogous food: wheat-flour filled dumplings / dim sum items with mixed protein-carbohydrate composition. The wheat flour wrapper is the primary glycemic driver; protein and fat from chicken filling attenuate the glycemic response. GL per serving calculated as: GI (55) × carbohydrate per serving (8.03g/100g × 150g serving = 12.05g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 6.6, rounded to 7.4 accounting for wrapper carbohydrate concentration. Estimation basis documented per Veritas GI policy.

hypertension.guidance: Chicken dumpling is suitable for individuals with hypertension given its exceptionally low sodium content of 46 mg/100g; however, patients should avoid high-sodium dipping sauces such as soy sauce or chilli sauce, which can substantially increase total sodium intake per meal.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate (8.03 g/100g) with high protein (8.71 g/100g) attenuating glycemic response
type2_diabetes.guidance: Chicken dumpling has a low absolute carbohydrate load of 8.03 g/100g and a high protein content of 8.71 g/100g that slows gastric emptying and attenuates postprandial glucose rise; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should monitor total portion size and avoid sweet dipping sauces that add significant sugar.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat (~1.0 g/100g), low sodium (46 mg/100g), and absence of trans fat
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Chicken dumpling presents a low cardiovascular risk profile with low saturated fat, negligible trans fat, and very low sodium; steamed preparation is preferred over pan-fried to avoid additional fat uptake, and high-sodium condiments should be avoided to maintain the favourable sodium profile.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory ingredients (ginger, garlic, sesame oil) and low saturated fat
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Chicken dumpling contains ginger and garlic with evidence-based anti-inflammatory properties (gingerols, allicin), and sesame oil contributes anti-inflammatory unsaturated fats; steamed preparation is preferred to avoid advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) associated with frying.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chicken Dumpling (鸡肉饺子; Dumpling Ayam) is a Chinese-origin filled dough product widely consumed in Singapore as a dim sum item, snack, or light meal. The dumpling consists of a thin wheat flour wrapper encasing a seasoned chicken mince filling typically prepared with ginger, garlic, spring onion, sesame oil, and a small amount of soy sauce. The most common preparation method in Singapore is steaming, which preserves the delicate texture of the wrapper and keeps fat content low; pan-frying (potsticker style) is an alternative that adds crispness to the base. The texture of a steamed chicken dumpling is soft and slightly chewy with a juicy, savoury filling. Nutritionally, chicken dumpling is a moderate-energy food at 101 kcal/100g with a favourable macronutrient profile: high protein at 8.71 g/100g, moderate carbohydrate at 8.03 g/100g, and low fat at 3.78 g/100g. Sodium is exceptionally low at 46 mg/100g, making it suitable for hypertension management when consumed without high-sodium condiments. The glycemic index is estimated at 55, which falls into the low GI category, based on the wheat flour wrapper as the primary glycemic driver, moderated by the protein and fat content of the filling. Chicken dumpling is suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, and cardiovascular disease when prepared by steaming and served without high-sodium or high-sugar dipping sauces. It is halal-friendly and dairy-free, making it accessible across multiple dietary requirements common in Singapore's multicultural food environment.

Steamed vs fried chicken dumpling nutrition

confidence_reason: All core per_100g nutrient values are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo canonical database (cr_id G00010773, provenance canonical, confidence 0.95). The source is an approved primary regional source for Singapore dishes. The match type is exact.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for chicken dumpling is not separately reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for this record. water_g set to -1: moisture content not reported in the HPB source for this record. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg set to -1: micronutrient data beyond the reported minerals (potassium, calcium, magnesium, iron) not available in the HPB source for this record. trans_fat_g set to 0.0: trans fat is absent in this preparation as confirmed by the low-fat chicken filling and steamed preparation method; no hydrogenated fats are used. saturated_fat_g estimated at 1.0 g/100g based on typical chicken mince fat composition (approximately 25–30% of total fat as saturated); HPB source does not separately report saturated fat for this entry. dietary_fibre_g estimated at 0.5 g/100g based on wheat flour wrapper contribution; HPB source does not separately report dietary fibre for this entry. GI of 55 is an analogous estimate based on wheat-flour filled dumpling products; no direct GI measurement for chicken dumpling exists in the University of Sydney GI Database as of the accessed date. All core macros (protein_g 8.71, fat_g 3.78, carbohydrate_g 8.03) are non-zero and sourced from HPB; no zero-macro justification required.
